text: The surgeon uses a robotic system with color-coded instrument base units, placing two 5mm ports on the right and one on the left side of the chest. The Maryland instrument is positioned on the right hand, while the bipolar grasper is on the left. The surgeon grasps the right upper lobe to locate an apical nodule, preparing for a wedge resection for diagnostic purposes and potential lymphadenectomy.

The surgeon uses a grasper and Maryland dissector to locate the tumor nodule at the top of the chest. The lung is collapsed using CO2 insufflation at 6-8 mmHg for better visualization. The camera is positioned at the eighth intercostal space, providing a clear view of the nodule. The assistant introduces a Cavidian purple tri-stapler through a 12 mm port to perform the wedge resection.

The robotic arms stabilize the sample while the assistant uses a purple tri-stapler to complete the wedge resection under vision. The resected tissue is left in the chest to maintain the CO2 seal. The surgeon retracts the hilum anteriorly for a clear view of the posterior hilum and subcranial station, with the azygos vein visible at the top of the dissection. The incision is made down the back of the lung, with the lung tissue as the right-sided margin and the vagus nerve as the left-sided margin, dissecting over the bronchus intermedius.

The surgeon performs a lymph node dissection using a bipolar Maryland instrument, focusing on the bronchi, including the left-sided bronchi. The vagus nerve is visible on the left side, with its branches managed using the bipolar instrument. The bronchus intermedius is pulled back by the Maryland, while the grasper holds the station seven lymph node. The dissection proceeds close to the bronchus intermedius, aiming to dissect the subcarinal station up to the carina.

The Versius robot is used to remove the station seven lymph node from the subcarinal station, utilizing a glove thumb technique through the assistant port. The surgeon meticulously checks for any remaining lymph nodes, including those tucked under the bronchus intermedius, ensuring complete removal.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the subcarinal station, leaving the lymph node visible. The left main bronchus is positioned under the bipolar grasper. The subcarinal station is emptied, revealing the bronchial artery originating from the descending aorta near the carina. The artery is managed with pressure or diathermy. The final part of the subcarinal station is removed using the thumb of a glove technique. The hilum is pulled posteriorly, and the lung is pushed back to expose the anterior station ten lymph nodes, followed by exploration of the station two or four lymph nodes.

The surgeon identifies the azygos vein and superior vena cava (SVC) and begins the dissection of station II-R and IV-R lymph nodes above the azygos vein. The pleura is stripped along the SVC, exposing the area for lymph node removal. The bipolar instrument is used for hemostatic dissection, effectively clamping smaller branches. A IV-R lymph node is extracted above the azygos vein using the thumb of a glove technique. The surgeon proceeds to dissect anterior station XR lymph nodes below the azygos, ensuring thorough removal from areas where tumors may drain.

The surgeon completes the lymphadenectomy using the Versius robotic system, ensuring the subcarinal station is empty of lymph nodes. The left main bronchus and carina are clearly visible, with no lymph nodes remaining. The back of the left atrium is visible to the right of the Maryland grasper. Swabs are removed, and the wedge resection is taken out in a bag to maintain the CO2 seal.
